factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies the applicable industry classification as “ANZSIC 2221 Structural Steel Fabricating” in the industry definition section and cites ANZSIC 2006 Class 2221.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides employment figures for multiple years within 2018–2024 that fall within the 4,200–4,600 range, including 2020 (~4,600) and 2021 (~4,500). It also lists 2023 and 2024 at ~4,100, which are outside the required range, but the criterion only requires in-range figures for multiple years.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states “2024 Market Revenue” as NZD 1.8 billion and discusses the 2023–2024 period, which falls within the required approximate NZD 1.4–1.9 billion range for 2023–2024.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that installed sector capacity is “120,000–150,000 tonnes” as of 2023–2024 in the Tonnage Fabricated section, which matches the required domestic fabrication capacity range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides geographic locations for multiple major fabricators, including Whangārei (Culham Engineering), Auckland (D&H Steel Construction and Culham regional office), Christchurch and Timaru (John Jones Steel), plus Mt Maunganui and Napier for others.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies the applicable tariff codes as HS 7207–7216 in the import tariff section, describing them as covering key raw/structural steel inputs.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says most HS 7207–7216 structural steel inputs are duty-free, but it does not specifically state that this is under the NZ Normal Tariff for HS 7207–7216 nor does it note that some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates. It instead broadly mentions a 5% non-preferential rate without tying it to specific 7216 lines.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives City Rail Link steel demand as “~25,000 tonnes of locally produced reinforcing steel,” which is outside the required approximately 30,000–32,000 tonnes reinforcing range and does not provide the alternative 3,000–5,000 tonnes structural figure. Therefore it does not state the specified CRL steel demand values.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states for City Rail Link: “operational launch scheduled for second half of 2026,” which places completion/opening circa 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not mention Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel tonnage at all. There is no statement giving an approximate figure of 9,000 tonnes for the stadium roof steel, nor any equivalent phrasing or estimate.

The response does not mention Te Kaha Stadium at all and provides no statement about its opening year. It lists three infrastructure projects (City Rail Link, Transmission Gully, Waikato Green Steel) but omits Te Kaha Stadium and therefore does not state an opening in 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists three major infrastructure projects (City Rail Link, Transmission Gully, Waikato Green Steel plant) but does not mention New Dunedin Hospital anywhere or describe it as a major project requiring significant structural steel components.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes revenue discrepancies and attributes them to scope/definition differences (ANZSIC 2221 narrow fabrication vs broader metalworks/value-chain reports), and reiterates preference for the narrower boundary. It also mentions mismatched inclusion of fabrication vs broader metalworks as a cause of differing figures.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states the Top 5 list includes “true fabricators” and excludes distributors/mills (e.g., Steel & Tube, New Zealand Steel, Pacific Steel), noting they are deliberately excluded from rankings.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains that because most structural steel inputs are effectively duty-free, tariffs don’t provide meaningful border protection, so fabricator margins are driven instead by global steel price swings, freight/shipping costs, and exchange-rate movements.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response references ANZSIC 2221 and mentions Stats NZ/MBIE sources, but it does not provide an actionable, step-by-step method for extracting class-level data (e.g., downloading the Stats NZ AES CSV and filtering by ANZSIC L4 code C2221). No concrete instructions, dataset/table name, or filtering procedure is given.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses construction-cycle volatility and a weakening 2024–2025 pipeline with a rebound forecast from 2026, linking market conditions to cycle timing. However, it does not explicitly connect specific employment or revenue peaks/troughs to a dated construction slowdown (e.g., a 2023 peak preceding a mid-2022 slowdown).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response gives some project steel tonnage for City Rail Link but does not aggregate steel demand across multiple named projects with explicit tonnages to validate fabrication capacity/throughput. Transmission Gully has no quantified tonnage and the Waikato project cites processing capacity rather than project steel demand. No summed total is compared against stated fabrication capacity.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes gaps in an official annual tonnage series (“Tonnage data gaps”) and describes a triangulation method using SCNZ capacity/utilization reports and IBISWorld volumes as anchor points. This constitutes a specific triangulation approach leveraging SCNZ capacity data and other disclosures to estimate tonnage.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly concludes that import tariffs are effectively 0% and therefore do not significantly affect fabricator margins, instead attributing margin volatility to global steel prices, freight, and exchange rates. It does not blame NZ import tariffs or border protection for competitive challenges.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: Some quantitative figures include units (e.g., NZD 1.8 billion, 100,000 tonnes, 0%/5% tariffs) and years, but many are missing explicit timeframes or precision (e.g., “multiple thousands of tonnes,” “~NZ$25m,” “4,100–5,000 people,” “CAGR –6% during 2020–2025”). The response does not consistently present all quantitative data with explicit units, specific FY/year labels, and clear measurement precision.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly organized into numbered sections that separately cover market size/growth (Section 2), companies (Section 3), tariffs (Section 4), projects (Section 5), and risks/opportunities (Section 6).

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is written in a generally professional, analytical tone and avoids overtly promotional phrasing. However, it includes speculative/unsupported forward-looking claims (e.g., forecasts to 2026–2029, “expert consensus,” and interpolated trendlines) that are not fully supported by the cited official sources.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response provides some year-specific employment figures (e.g., 2018, 2020, 2021, 2023, 2024) but does not present 2018–2024 historical employment, revenue, or capacity data in a table or structured year-by-year format. Revenue and tonnage/capacity are mostly given as single-point estimates or ranges rather than a complete annual series. No table covering 2018–2024 is included.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-style tone throughout, using structured headings and technical language. It does not include rhetorical questions or notably colloquial/informal phrasing.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is highly verbose, adding lengthy methodology, reconciliation notes, multipliers, and extended narrative sections that go beyond the requested deliverables. It includes filler/expansive framing (e.g., scope boundaries, triangulation, discrepancies) and extra subsections that increase length without directly providing the specified 2018–2024 official series and ranked top-5 evidence.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response references Stats NZ generally but does not cite Stats NZ Business Demography (or an equivalent named Stats NZ publication) as the source for employment figures. The employment numbers are attributed mainly to IBISWorld and SCNZ, with no Business Demography hyperlink or publication date provided for employment.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response references Stats NZ generally but does not cite the Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or any specific Stats NZ economic survey publication/table for revenue or sales data, nor provide an AES hyperlink/date.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites NZ Customs’ official “Working Tariff Document” and provides a hyperlink to the Customs tariff schedule page as the source for HS 7207–7216 duty rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) multiple times as an industry association source for fabrication capacity/throughput, including citing SCNZ Industry Updates and SCNZ pages on local industry and statistics.

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response references Companies Office as a data source multiple times, including in the fabricator profiles and the sources list (e.g., “Companies Office – Culham Engineering” and links to companiesoffice.govt.nz). This satisfies the criterion’s requirement to reference NZ Companies Office.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites specific sources for CRL (NZ Steel article and CRL/KiwiRail pages) and for Transmission Gully (Infrastructure NZ report and the Auditor-General review), satisfying the requirement for at least two major infrastructure projects with cited data sources.
